# Striving for Nuclear Armageddon — Able Archer 1983 & today — Theodore Postol / Glenn Diesen (digest)

**Source:** YouTube — **Glenn Diesen** (Greater Eurasia / associated channel) interviewing **Theodore Postol** (MIT; nuclear weapons effects, BMD, targeting).

**Watch URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2GbJg8bb4Dg — *confirm in player; title commonly indexed as “Theodore Postol: Striving for Nuclear Armageddon.”*

**Air date (approx.):** **2025-03** (aligns with public listing for the above id; **verify**).

**Ingested:** 2026-04-02 (operator **`strategy postol` ingest**).

**Scope:** [work-strategy](../../../../docs/skill-work/work-strategy/README.md) upstream — historical exercise reconstruction + contemporary policy (Germany **2026** missiles, **INF**, **ABM**, **Poseidon**, civil effects pedagogy). **Not** companion Record.

**ASR note:** Captions render **Abel** / **Pol** / **posidon** / **egi** etc. — normalize **Able Archer**, **Postol**, **Poseidon**, **Aegis** in formal cites.

**Operator resource:** Speaker offers **PDF slides** by email — transcript ASR **“p s l @ mit.edu”**; almost certainly **postol@mit.edu** (**verify** before sending). Subject line: **Able Archer briefing**; separate email titled **super fires article** for the 1986 National Academy piece.

---

## Perceiver — neutral summary (≤200 words)

Postol uses **NATO exercise Able Archer (1983)** as a **staged escalation narrative**: **Blue** first uses **tactical nuclear artillery** (~**10–15 kt**) in **West Germany** against a **postulated Soviet conventional advance**; **Red** responds; over **days** the scenario ramps to **theater** then **strategic** yields (**~100 kt**, **~1 Mt**) on **German, Dutch, Polish**, then **European capitals** and **global** strikes, ending in **general nuclear war** by **day five**. He stresses **three weapons effects** — **thermal flash / mass fire** (dominant urban killer in his framing), **prompt fallout / downwind plume** (compares activity to **Chernobyl** at stated multiples and times), **blast** (important but “least” of three for survival); **military planners** face pressure to **strike counterforce** early. He argues the **1983 Soviet invasion scenario** was **invented** and **Russian capability today** does not support a **sweep-West-Europe** story without **decades** of visible buildup. **Contemporary hooks:** **U.S./Germany** **theater missiles** in **Germany from 2026** — he asserts they **must be nuclear** to be militarily meaningful; **mirrors 1983** ignition risk; plans to speak in **Germany (March 24)**. **ABM Treaty** collapse (**2002**) and **missile defense** as **perception driver** and **Putin** concern (**American ignorance** vs technical performance). **Poseidon** as **undeterrable-by-BMD** signal. Criticizes **Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists** and **European “Hitler” framing**; anecdote **UN** exchange with **German** representative.

---

## Strategy hooks (for lenses / STRATEGY §IV)

| Thread | One line |
|--------|----------|
| **Able Archer pedagogy** | **Exercise timeline** as **escalation physics** + **crisis misperception** (Soviet fear of **cover first strike** — raised at close). |
| **Effects hierarchy** | **Fire + fallout vs blast** — ties to **bridge** example (**Nagasaki** bridge standing → **higher yield** for **military** goals). |
| **Germany 2026** | **Theater missiles** claimed **nuclear-necessary**; **INF** reinstatement / **cancel deployment** as policy fork. |
| **BMD / stability** | **ABM** logic, **Aegis vs GMD**, **Putin** reads **U.S. decision-maker delusion** not **intercept physics**. |
| **Poseidon** | **Second-strike visibility** to **uninformed** leadership — interpret as **signal**, verify **yield** claims. |
| **Governance / expertise** | **Targeting officers** without **effects** training; **political scientists** vs **effects facts** — **normative** edge. |
| **Alliance narrative** | **European Russia-as-Hitler** discourse vs **capability reality** — **work-politics** only with sources. |

---

expands out outward and that compressed shell of air then propagates on its own outward as shown by this line here so this is what's called the primary shock there's a secondary shock depending on the height of burst that's generated uh from the primary Shock by reflection off the ground in this particular diagram this is the reflected shock and the reason you might adjust the altitude is because the uh secondary shock and the primary shock coales into a a shock front called the mock front and by carefully adjusting

higher speed than the shock wave that's the combination of the primary and secondary shock but that's because the shock wave in the ground travels faster than the shock wave in air so the air slap is causing uh this material to blow off on the ground from the explosive effects of of the shock wave that's propagating through the ground and an effect called thermal popcorn Corning uh the light and heat from the fireball at this distance and time from uh the nuclear explosion is probably uh about a 100,000 times brighter than the
sun in the desert at noon this is occurring at a very short time and what happens is at this when the the rate of heating is so high that grains of sand on the ground are are heated at such a high rate that the heat at the surface the temperature at the surface is enormously higher than the temperature in the interior because the heat is coming in so fast and because it Heats at the surface so so quickly and it's a brittle material it just pops into dust into thermally heated dust and that's what you're seeing here this is called
thermal pop Corning if you know what popcorn is uh it's it's a it's it's an effect where the particles just explode thermally and here you can see the the uh Fireball continuing to expand and I won't spend a lot of detail on this here again you have the distance has changed quite a bit notice that the stem is quite broad now because you have the Circ cating uh Rising hot air uh from the detonation itself keep in mind that this is an explosion a test explosion in the desert so you do not have combusting material

wave lasts fractions of a second to seconds in fact it tends to last a second or more 1 second is a long time uh to for for this thing because the speed of sound is uh 1100 ft per second so it's uh three uh 300 meters per second so so if it's passing over you and the shell is uh is hundreds of meters in in in in in in in width it's going to it's going to be pressing on the entire object uh for periods of tenths of seconds to seconds so here is a sequence of events so keep in mind that what we're showing now is we're seeing the initial light
from the fireball so um the fireball is not yet as bright as it will be so it's going to get brighter and this is going to occur in this particular case in a few ten of seconds because this is a low yield nuclear weapon test this is done uh in by the United States so now we see this is this is at about one or two1 of a second after the fireball has lit up what you're seeing now is the uh front of the building burning off of course the light would shine in through windows and it would set off curtains interior uh structures uh uh holstery um
uh chairs um you know all kinds of internal so so this place would be on fire in the following seconds uh that um where I'll be showing you the effects coming then in this particular case it would probably be uh maybe a couple of seconds later so so we're talking about the first few ten of a seconds here the fireball has expanded it is not yet finished producing the shock wave at this point so we're just seeing the light of the fireball ahead of the shock wave that it's still producing so then in about two seconds uh we're not yet seeing the
shock wave and then the shock wave arrives notice that the shock wve is tearing the top here's the front of the shock wave that's this is about 5 pounds per square inch and notice that it's B the the the front walls of the building are are buckling this is as the shock wave arrives but the shock wave is going going to envelop the whole structure so here we see the shock wave beginning to envelop the structure and you can see the top of the roof is coming off because it's accompanied the shock wave is accompanied by 200 m per hour winds so
the front of the building is buckling from the initial shock the winds are beginning to tear the building apart and we can see that as the as the shock wave continues to pass by the the structure the it looks like the top of the building has been hit from the top by a gigantic Mallet you can see the the uh roof is just uh caving upward in both cases uh by the crushing effect and the and the wind motion and then as we look forward the building just explosively disintegrates now in this case this is what the building looks like after the
test so you say well why is anything still there well nothing would be there in a normal situation because um this thing would have then burnt in a violent fire that would have lasted for hours in the city you'd see nothing but Ash uh in its location but this was a test in the desert so this building was covered uh with asbestos shingles there were no internal materials to catch fire because that was intentionally removed because the purpose of the experiment was to measure the effects of blast on the building in
fact the effects of fire were ignored for decades in the assessments of of the effects of nuclear uh uh nuclear weapons on cities this did not come into the Forefront of of American awareness awareness of the public until I wrote a paper in 19 uh I think it was 86 actually uh called um the effects uh of of superf fires on civilian uh Targets this effect was ignored until it became advertised through this article which was widely circulated because I presented this as the lead article in a National Academy of medicine uh uh for uh Forum in
Washington it got into the newspapers it was discussed in uh in the Congress and people started asking questions at the Congressional level why do the civil defense people not include fires so this had a major effect I'm glad to say on the perception of the true effects of nuclear weapons on our society but uh and I when I say our society I mean Western Europe as well and there's a lesson here for your audience to keep very clearly understood that this effect is known now is now widely understood because of the accident of my
opportunity to present a detailed and clear discussion of this effect in a forum that was widely being monitored by Leading people in our society a National Academy of Medicine uh Forum otherwise it would be ignored and the general public would still be being misled into believe that these believing that these nuclear weapons are much less lethal than they actually are those of you who want copies of that article also send an email please send a separate email though uh title the article super fires article and I will
send you a copy of that article from the National Academy of Medicine study and it describes in great detail uh the effects of these super fires and it made a big difference I'm very pleased to say but I also don't want to Pat myself on the back too much because it was an accident of circumstances okay so the unique effects of of the extremely intense light and heat from the fireball have only in part been described I just want to describe those before I end my discussion and give you some sense of how shocking these effects
are they're shocking because they're outside of Human Experience so you think you understand that this intense flash of light and heat is a bad thing of course that's easy to say but it's it's much more shocking to understand some of the actual physical consequences I'd like to show you those and then I'll end it there but my purpose of showing you this is to give you is to give you a more visceral sense of these pH these effects so that you won't forget about them because it's easy to focus on the blast effects because that's something
we think we understand but the fire effects are far more consequential so so what I'm talking about is the intense light and heat from the fireball so here's an example of what occurred at Hiroshima uh the this is is not will not give you a visceral reaction even unless you're a person who understands the character of what you're looking at and most people wouldn't but in this case uh these this was a tile where there were other tiles on top of it and the the part of the tile that was exposed to the light and heat from the

that's because the brick the surface of the brick kind of exploded off from the exposure to the intense light and heat in a similar way to the to the um uh uh slate uh the piece of slate I showed you earlier but in this case it's a much more dramatic example of what the light and heat from the fireball can do to people in a Target area here is yet another example again what you're seeing uh it's less dramatic but should be at this point I hope shocking uh because what you see is the blistered paint on the on the metal
wall of of behind this um valve stem and the valve stem produced a shadow from the Fireball and um and because of the Shadow uh the paint was not blistered immediately uh behind uh the valve stem this was how the early people um uh early the Japanese scientists who came in early after the atomic bombing in 1945 determined the height a burst of the nuclear weapon I was privileged to know a man modura Kamura I met Modo in in his senior years and he was involved in measuring the the height of the atomic bomb I met him when he was
uh visiting my laboratory when I was still doing science visiting from Japan a wonderful man who uh who taught me a lot from his personal experiences as a scientist in Japan here is another shocking example of the effects of light and heat from the fireball I don't think I need to describe it much to you again the wall the paint on the wall uh was blistered but uh the ladder and the person standing in front uh intercepted the light and of course the person was severely burnt and certainly died after the attack
here is another shocking example of how the light from the fireball can affect an object in this particular case we're looking at a material that's obviously light and dark material strips of dark material and strips of light material now the fire the light from the fireball when it uh when it when it fell on the black material at certain points caused the black material to the the the fact that the material was black meant it preferentially absorbed the light from the Fireball and burst into flames while the white
material reflected enough light that it did not burst into flames so here is an effect where you can see the actual um color of the material had an effect on the way the material uh reacted to the bright Fireball in a in a way that you can understand but of course is shocking or should be shocking if you weren't aware of this kind of effect here again you see this effect on sheer white material uh this material was covered with um little flowers I I think the next slide shows them yeah here here's a flower up
close and here the flowers were like were preferentially burnt out the material surrounding the flowers was lighter uh and a very light material and white and so it reflected enough light and heat that the material did not raise to a temperature where it burnt but the flowers were dark enough in color that they were selectively uh ignited and burnt out from the material this is almost a um uh I don't I don't know uh uh an i an image of evil you know if if one wants to take it that way uh and U and here of course are the
consequences of a woman who had some kind of material with a pattern on her covering her body when the weapon went off and the material preferentially caught flames and burnt her on her skin and you can see uh that some parts of the material reflected enough light and heat that she wasn't burned but of course you can see she was severely burnt this unfortunate woman would have certainly died from the combined effects of severe burns on her body which lead to infection and uh death and radiation exposure the flash of radiation gamma

she must have been carrying something and the strap was thick enough across her shoulder and and her back uh that uh uh she was uh protected from the effects so just to reemphasize the effects this is Nagasaki before the explosion and here it is after the explosion note again from the earliest part of this talk if you were looking at this from the point of view of a military planner you would say my goodness the bridge is still there the Soviets can still use that we need to hit that bridge and it's the fact that these
blast and radiation and thermal effects are all distinctly different in their scale but all there leads to this horrendous Hobson's choice that a military planner has to face even if they are not crazy and even if they are well informed of nuclear effects so for those of you who want to understand my psychology me as an individual this is the image that immediately jumps to my mind when I hear about people talking about nuclear war the people who talk about nuclear war who know nothing about their physical effects they have no idea what they're
talking about I want to I don't mean this to insulting but anybody who talks about nuclear war as a tolerable thing to deal with is simply uninformed and if they haven't taken the trouble to understand the facts that I put forward in this briefing and they are in a decision-making role a political decision-making role I make the Judgment that's my personal judgment that these people are irresponsible and they shouldn't be allowed to be in a decision-making role if they haven't taken the trouble to inform themselves

referred to as uh as security dilemma sensibility that is the ability to put oursel in the shoes of the opponents and see the the threat from their eyes well I I give I give Reagan uh very high marks for uh understanding that he was dealing with something that was existential uh which he did not understand earlier but I don't blame I blame the system for not informing him of the existential effects uh that uh that would accompany uh any use of nuclear weapons because of the rapid escalation that is essentially
guaranteed because of the nature of nuclear weapons I give the system a zero a zero minus Mark and this is my concern today and uh that's why we are having this exercise you uh asked me to speak about this because we agreed that uh this is a a problem you recognize this problem and we agreed we both agreed that this is a problem but the real problem is that the systems that support political leadership our supposed intelligence people our supposed military uh uh advisers do not tell these people in decision-making roles what they need to
know everybody who has any decision Mak role at all with regard to nuclear weapons should see the talk I just gave you should have let me give you an example of of of a very disturbing personal experience I have had multiple times I have given talks not quite this talk but talks about nuclear weapons consequences I used to give this talk uh not this talk but a a talk similar uh at Stanford University when I was teaching there before I went to MIT and um it was a very large audience at the time I was giving this talk there
was a lot of interest in Arms Control this was the 1980s when there was Global public interest we had hundreds of students G at our lecture every year and um every year I would have one or two soldiers who were on leave at Stanford come up after the after the uh lecture and tell me that they were involved in nuclear targeting uh you know that they had been involved in nuclear targeting and they had no idea what the secondary the primary effects of these weapons really were they were were at um Omaha Nebraska the Strategic Air Command
headquarters they were involved in targeting I knew Strategic Air Command headquarters because when I was with the Navy before I went to Stanford I had responsibilities that took me out to Strategic Air Command and I was aware that the soldiers doing the target doing the targeting were basically uh being told you know here's a little circle and put it you know figure out where to put it between these targets they didn't know what the effects of the nuclear weapons were they had this little circle that they were asked to
place on the target they had no idea what the consequences of the nuclear uh strikes would actually be and to the credit of these young and moral soldiers when they saw the con consequences they felt compelled to come up after my talk and talk with me and I had nothing but sympathy for them and I you know I I comforted them as best I could I said look you were doing your job as a soldier that's the system we have I will be the first to tell you that this system is inadequate that's why I'm that at Stanford now and no longer in the
Pentagon I chose to leave because I want the freedom to be able to inform the public about the consequences of these nuclear weapons and that's all I did for my first few years in the public forum it was later that I started talking about missile defense and all these other important issues um because that's was my job as a scholar on these matters but my early days after I got out of the system got out from under its Yol was was to try to inform people soldiers as well as um as civilians about the horrendous
consequences of these weapons and uh I I I know that the soldiers I worked with did not have any idea of what they were doing and I don't blame them for it any more than I blame Ronald Reagan you know the system does not inform them and this is one of the things that political scientists who talk about the prisoners dilemma like I hear at Stanford this nonsense uh these guys talk about they have no idea about what they're talking about I I'm not talking about you Glenn because I know that you are a serious political scientist but I deal with
these people at Stanford who are theoreticians they don't learn the facts but they have theories and they don't understand what the consequences of nuclear use are but they have the prisoners dilemma and blah blah blah blah blah blah and they don't understand what they're doing and they don't choose to inform themselves and this is one of the greatest dangers of the American Political Science establishment European political scientists are of a different culture the scholarly culture in Europe is different although I'm afraid you have your victims of that culture too but nothing like you see in the United States and these people particularly at Stanford here think they are the highest form of political theorist you know John mirima he's just he's a dirty guy he has just dirt all over his his his his feet you know doing looking at details but they are above it all you know and these people are in my view among the most dangerous people because they talk to political decision makers and this is what we have in the top tier of the US government these

from a well defensive weapon a for the turns to more of an offensive weapon what do you see where do the main threats come from uh over the next few years well I I I I I think all of the above I'm I'm sorry to say um uh the American preoccupation with missile defenses uh has uh two disastrous consequences uh actually we have begun to we are seeing the disastrous consequences as of 2002 when the Bush Administration uh withdrew from the ABM Treaty of 1972 and uh in essence the Bush Administration uh destroyed the whole
structure upon which arms limitations was constructed in the second half of the uh 20th century because the ABM Treaty of 1972 uh was critical to the uh arms limitation treaties that were that followed that includes the intermediate nuclear forces treaty uh and the the Strategic arms treaties and and the start treaties and all these treaties because the ABM Treaty of 1972 explicitly right in its in its Preamble talks about the fact that that missile defenses create the perception that an adversary might be able to
strike and then respond by defending themselves against retaliation and the perception that this could be possible is enough to cause everybody who is looking at missile defenses of the other side to respond by expanding their nuclear their offensive nuclear arsenals so the ABM Treaty was critical to limiting missile defenses to a level where neither side could in any way anticipate any utility to those missile defenses and thereby they could comfortably engage in nuclear offensive arms reductions this was this was the
Holy Grail for arms reductions this was the key to Arms reductions now moving ahead with American missile defenses which we have done after abating the ABM Treaty and I include my Democratic friends I want to who who made it far worse than the Republicans made it because the egis system is a far worse decision than the decision to build the groundbased missile defense was because the egis system has all these interceptors all of which can do nothing nothing the EEG system is worthless as a technical capability worthless but if
you're a Russian military planner you look at it and you say what are the Americans going to do next they're building a foundation and I don't know what they're going to do with that Foundation at some future time and of Greater importance than the capability if you listen extremely carefully to Vladimir Putin and I listen carefully to Putin when he speaks I I'm I'm really up there with my ear to the word for word because I really want to try to understand what's going through his mind on that sub on the subject of
nuclear retaliation and the message I get from listening carefully to Putin is he's not afraid that the Americans can build a missile defense with any capability he's afraid that a bonehead American political decision maker could think that these missile defenses we have have some capability and take an action that could lead Russia to have no choice but to retaliate and the Russian retaliation he understands would result in the destruction of Russia along with the United States so his main fear is American ignorance combined
with missile defense and he he's smart enough and well informed enough in my judgment from everything I've heard him say to understand that these missile defenses will never provide any capability but he is not sure that an American political decision maker is well informed enough that he won't be misled into believing that they have some capability and that is the same outcome as a mistake leading to a nuclear exchange that ends both Russia and the United States and civilization so he understands these missile defenses so
